title: NFT ownership disputes - how Indian courts might approach them
p1:
You bought an NFT. You hold the digital token. But do you actually own the artwork behind it?

This question may become one of the most interesting legal challenges as digital assets enter mainstream business and investment conversations.
p2:
A common misconception is that owning an NFT automatically means owning all rights connected with it.

In reality, an NFT may prove ownership of a token on a blockchain, but copyright, licensing, and commercial rights can remain with someone else. Indian courts may have to separate these different layers of ownership while applying existing intellectual property principles.
p3:
The bigger challenge will be adapting traditional legal concepts to blockchain technology. Courts may examine contracts, terms of sale, creator rights, and the intention of parties rather than relying only on the digital record.

The key takeaway: blockchain can show who holds a token, but law decides what that ownership actually means.
conclusion: If an NFT dispute reaches Indian courts, should ownership be decided mainly by blockchain records or by traditional contract and copyright principles?
